reasoning: TIME's Person of the Year in non-election years usually features a wide, unpredictable slate of candidates (e.g., social movements, foreign leaders, scientists, or cultural figures). The base rate for any specific individual winning a priori is less than 5%. Moreover, Elon Musk already won the title in 2021. Historically, TIME rarely selects the same person twice unless they are a U.S. President winning re-election or a pivotal wartime world leader. Awarding Person of the Year to a non-head-of-state / business figure twice within a span of four years is essentially without precedent. Without search access to any breaking news up to October 2025 that might uniquely elevate him, I must rely heavily on these strong historical base rates. Therefore, the likelihood of him being selected again for 2025 is very low. I estimate it at roughly 4%.
